prompt: As of June 2026, what is the state of the art in LLM knowledge distillation, especially DISTILLING FROM A BLACK-BOX API TEACHER (OpenAI/Anthropic, no logits) into a small open student? Cover: on-policy distillation (GKD, on-policy KD), black-box / sequence-level KD, reverse-KL (MiniLLM), rejection sampling / best-of-n distillation, distilling reasoning/CoT from reasoning models, and any 2025-2026 methods that beat plain SFT-on-teacher-outputs. Cite specific papers (arXiv id + date), GitHub repos, and benchmark deltas. Rank the approaches. Then state concretely: for a startup that captures a customer's real OpenAI/Anthropic API traffic and distills it into their own small model, which 2-3 methods give the biggest quality jump and are implementable without teacher logits? Be specific and numeric.
content:
**As of June 2026, the state of the art for black-box LLM knowledge distillation (teacher API access only: text outputs/responses, no logits, parameters, or tokenizer compatibility required) emphasizes on-policy methods that mitigate exposure bias and distribution mismatch inherent in plain supervised fine-tuning (SFT/SeqKD) on teacher outputs.**[[1]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.10643)[[2]](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.10643v1)

Plain **SeqKD/SFT** (generate teacher responses on a prompt set → SFT student) remains the simple baseline but is consistently outperformed by on-policy approaches, which train on student-generated samples with teacher or derived feedback. Key advances focus on stable on-policy signals without logits (e.g., via discriminators, rubrics, or multi-sample selection).

### Key Approaches and Ranking
Here is a ranked overview (strongest for black-box first, based on recency, empirical gains over SeqKD, and applicability; citations include arXiv IDs + dates where available):

1. **GAD (Generative Adversarial Distillation)** — Top for black-box on-policy (Nov 2025). arXiv:2511.10643 (Ye et al., Nov 13, 2025; Microsoft Research). Frames student as generator in a minimax game with a discriminator (trained to distinguish student vs. teacher responses). The discriminator serves as a co-evolving, on-policy reward model providing adaptive sequence-level feedback. Enables true on-policy distillation from proprietary APIs (incompatible vocabularies OK). Open-sources code/data/models.[[1]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.10643)[[3]](https://ytianzhu.github.io/Generative-Adversarial-Distillation/)

   - **Benchmarks**: Consistently beats SeqKD. Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B-Instruct students show robust gains (especially OOD: Dolly, SelfInst, Vicuna). Qwen2.5-14B + GAD reaches parity/comparability with GPT-5-Chat teacher on LMSYS-Chat (GPT-4o judge; e.g., ~51.7 teacher score range; student closes most of the gap vs. baseline instruct models ~45–49). Human eval confirms wins (>50% win rate). Stronger generalization than SeqKD (lower n-gram overlap, less memorization of surface patterns).[[2]](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.10643v1)[[4]](https://thesalt.substack.com/p/generative-adversarial-distillation)
   - **GitHub**: Project site links to open-sourced artifacts (ytianzhu.github.io/Generative-Adversarial-Distillation or related repos).

2. **GKD (Generalized/On-Policy Knowledge Distillation)** and variants — Foundational on-policy (2023, extended 2025–2026). arXiv:2306.13649 (Agarwal et al., ICLR 2024). Trains student on its own self-generated outputs using teacher feedback (e.g., token-level or sequence signals) to correct mistakes on-policy. Integrates with RLHF-style training.[[5]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.13649)

   - **Benchmarks**: Consistent improvements over SeqKD/Supervised KD on summarization, translation, reasoning, and instruction tuning. Later works (e.g., 2026 surveys/extensions like OPSD/BRTS) show further gains with best-of-N teacher rollouts or rubric-based signals.
   - **Related**: MiniLLM (reverse KL variant; arXiv:2306.08543, Gu et al., 2023/ICLR 2024) replaces forward KL with reverse KL + on-policy optimization to focus on teacher modes and avoid overestimating low-probability regions. Strong on generative quality.[[6]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.08543)

3. **Best-of-N / Rejection Sampling Distillation** (e.g., BOND, BRTS, RAFT-style) — Practical for quality filtering (2024–2026). Examples: BOND (arXiv:2407.14622, 2024; Jeffreys divergence mimicking BoN); BRTS (arXiv:2605.09725, 2026; best-of-N teacher rollouts with auxiliary context). Sample multiple student (or teacher) responses per prompt; select/reject via teacher scoring, consistency, or lightweight verifier.[[7]](https://github.com/chrisliu298/awesome-on-policy-distillation)

   - **Benchmarks**: Boosts alignment/quality; can eliminate inference-time BoN cost via distillation. Often combined with on-policy KD for 3–5+ point gains on reasoning/alignment evals vs. plain SFT. Efficient when leveraging real traffic prompts.

4. **GrayKD and rationale/CoT-focused methods** (2026). GrayKD (AAAI 2026, ~March 2026): Multi-rationale injection via lightweight cross-attention (teacher mode) to approximate teacher distribution in a single stage; then student-mode training. Bridges black-box gap with parameter-efficient modules.[[8]](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/402627228_GrayKD_Distilling_Better_Knowledge_from_Black-box_LLM_via_Multi-rationale_Injection)[[9]](https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/40470/44431)

   - **CoT/Reasoning distillation**: Sample multiple reasoning paths from teacher (or student), filter via self-consistency/rejection/best-of-N, or inject rationales. Recent works (2025–2026) show iterative or dual-criteria sampling outperforms uniform SFT, especially for math/reasoning (gains via higher-quality synthetic CoT data). Can be layered on GAD/GKD.

5. **Other 2025–2026 notables beating plain SFT**: DDK (domain-adaptive dataset composition, NeurIPS 2024 with 2025 extensions); TASKD-LLM (task-aware selective); rubric-based OPD (structured verbal feedback replacing logits). Surveys (e.g., arXiv:2604.00626, 2026) formalize on-policy axes.[[10]](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/hash/b206d54ffbb803b5c51d85f405d422e4-Abstract-Conference.html)

**Plain SeqKD/SFT** is the weakest for closing gaps (marginal OOD gains or regressions); on-policy/adversarial methods provide the biggest jumps via better generalization and reduced exposure bias.

### Recommendations for a Startup Capturing Real OpenAI/Anthropic API Traffic
Capture prompts + teacher responses from customer traffic (high-quality, in-distribution data). This is ideal for black-box distillation—no logits needed.

**Top 2–3 methods for biggest quality jump (implementable without logits; expect 2–5+ point gains on GPT-4o/LMSYS-style judges or reasoning benchmarks vs. plain SeqKD/SFT, depending on data volume/scale; e.g., 14B student nearing much larger teacher performance):**

1. **GAD (or close adversarial/on-policy variant)** — Biggest single jump for black-box. Use captured prompts; generate teacher responses if needed; train discriminator on student vs. teacher outputs; alternate generator (student policy gradient with discriminator rewards) and discriminator updates. Co-evolves reward signal adaptively. Start with warmup (SFT + discriminator). Highest generalization; open-source artifacts accelerate implementation. Numeric edge: Closes most of teacher gap where SeqKD leaves 3–5+ points.[[3]](https://ytianzhu.github.io/Generative-Adversarial-Distillation/)

2. **GKD + Best-of-N/Rejection Sampling** — Strong complementary or alternative. On-policy training on student samples with teacher feedback (or rubric/BoN selection of best responses). Layer rejection sampling (sample N responses per prompt from student/teacher; filter by quality/consistency/teacher preference) to curate higher-quality targets or rewards. Very practical with real traffic (reuse prompts for multiple samples). Numeric: Often +2–4+ over SeqKD on reasoning/alignment; combines well with GAD.[[7]](https://github.com/chrisliu298/awesome-on-policy-distillation)

3. **GrayKD-style rationale/CoT injection or MiniLLM reverse-KL on-policy** (if resources allow lightweight modules or KL approximation via sampling) — For reasoning-heavy traffic. Inject multi-rationale or use reverse KL on-policy. Good additive boost for CoT quality.

**Implementation notes**: Start with SeqKD baseline on captured pairs for quick wins, then layer on-policy (GAD/GKD) for the largest gains. Scale with ~10K–200K examples (real traffic helps). Use GPT-4o-as-judge or human prefs for validation. All avoid logits; focus on sequence-level or derived rewards. Monitor for mode collapse (adversarial helps). These can make a small open student competitive with the proprietary teacher on customer-like distributions.
